quiz_questions.question_text: What is a pseudo-force?

options.text: A real contact force between two objects that obeys Newton's Third Law
A fictitious force added in a non-inertial frame to make F = ma work
A gravitational force due to the mass of the Earth acting on a body
An electromagnetic force between charged particles in a magnetic field

quiz_questions.explanation: A pseudo-force (fictitious force) is not a real force caused by an interaction between objects. It is added when analyzing motion from a non-inertial (accelerating) reference frame so that Newton's Second Law (F = ma) still gives correct results within that frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is centrifugal force a real force?

options.text: Yes — it is a fundamental force
Yes — it acts in all frames
No — it is a fictitious force in a rotating frame
Yes — it is a contact force

quiz_questions.explanation: Centrifugal force is a pseudo-force that appears only in a rotating reference frame. It seems to push objects outward, but from an inertial frame, what actually happens is that objects tend to travel in straight lines (inertia) while the frame rotates around them.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which direction does the Coriolis force deflect objects in the Northern Hemisphere?

options.text: To the right
To the left
Upward
Downward

quiz_questions.explanation: The Coriolis force is a pseudo-force in Earth's rotating frame. In the Northern Hemisphere, it deflects moving objects to the right. In the Southern Hemisphere, it deflects them to the left. It affects wind patterns, ocean currents, and large-scale weather systems.

quiz_questions.question_text: Do pseudo-forces have action-reaction pairs?

options.text: Yes, always
No, never
At high speeds
In rotating frames

quiz_questions.explanation: Pseudo-forces do not have action-reaction pairs because they are not real forces caused by interactions between two objects. They are mathematical tools used to apply Newton's laws in non-inertial frames. Real forces always have identifiable sources and obey Newton's Third Law.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do you feel pushed backward when a car accelerates forward?

options.text: The seat pushes you back
Air resistance pushes you
Gravity increases
Pseudo-force in the non-inertial frame of the car

quiz_questions.explanation: In the car's accelerating (non-inertial) frame, you feel pushed backward. This is a pseudo-force (F_pseudo = -ma_frame). From an inertial frame outside, your body simply resists the change in motion due to inertia while the car accelerates forward beneath you.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a non-inertial reference frame?

options.text: A frame that is accelerating
Any frame at rest
A frame attached to Earth
Any frame moving at constant velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: A non-inertial reference frame is one that is accelerating (either linearly or rotationally). Newton's laws do not hold in their standard form in non-inertial frames unless pseudo-forces are introduced to account for the frame's acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What must you add to apply Newton's laws in an accelerating frame?

options.text: Pseudo-forces (fictitious forces)
More mass
Real forces
Extra dimensions

quiz_questions.explanation: To use F = ma in a non-inertial frame, we introduce pseudo-forces (fictitious forces). These compensate for the frame's own acceleration. The pseudo-force is F_pseudo = -ma_frame, opposite to the direction of the frame's acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an inertial reference frame?

options.text: A frame that is accelerating forward or rotating around an axis
A frame at rest or moving at constant velocity where Newton's laws hold
A frame that is only valid inside a laboratory on Earth's surface
A frame where all objects are required to be completely motionless

quiz_questions.explanation: An inertial reference frame is one that is not accelerating — it is either at rest or moving at constant velocity. Newton's laws work directly in inertial frames without needing to add any pseudo-forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for the pseudo-force in a linearly accelerating frame?

options.text: F_pseudo = ma_frame (same direction as acceleration)
F_pseudo = -ma_frame (opposite to the frame's acceleration)
F_pseudo = mv²/r (toward the center of rotation)
F_pseudo = mg (equal to the weight of the object)

quiz_questions.explanation: In a linearly accelerating frame, the pseudo-force on an object of mass m is F_pseudo = -ma_frame. The negative sign indicates it points opposite to the frame's acceleration. If a train accelerates forward, passengers feel a pseudo-force backward.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does the Coriolis effect apply to small everyday objects like a draining bathtub?

options.text: Yes — it determines which way all water drains everywhere
No — it is too weak for small scales; it mainly affects large weather systems
Yes — it makes all water spin clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere
No — the Coriolis effect only exists in outer space beyond the atmosphere

quiz_questions.explanation: The Coriolis effect is significant only for large-scale, long-duration motions like weather systems, ocean currents, and wind patterns. For small objects like draining bathtubs, other factors (basin shape, initial water motion) dominate; the Coriolis force is far too small to notice.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of a 'reference frame' in physics?

options.text: A coordinate system from which an observer measures position and motion
A physical structure like a wall or floor that objects rest against
A unit of measurement used to describe the speed of moving objects
A type of force that acts on objects only when they are in motion

quiz_questions.explanation: A reference frame is a coordinate system (viewpoint) from which an observer measures and describes the position, velocity, and acceleration of objects. Different observers in different reference frames may describe the same event differently.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'fictitious force' mean?

options.text: A force that appears in an accelerating frame but has no real physical source
A force that is simply too small to be measured by current instruments
A force that only exists in science fiction stories and not in real physics
A force that acts between two objects that are not in physical contact

quiz_questions.explanation: A fictitious force (pseudo-force) is an apparent force that appears when observing motion from a non-inertial (accelerating) reference frame. It has no real physical source — no object is causing it. It is a mathematical tool to make Newton's laws work in accelerating frames.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'centrifugal force' mean?

options.text: An apparent outward force felt by objects in a rotating reference frame
The real inward force that keeps objects moving in a circular path
The force of gravity pulling an object toward the center of the Earth
The friction force between a spinning wheel and the ground beneath

quiz_questions.explanation: Centrifugal force is a pseudo-force that appears in a rotating reference frame. It seems to push objects outward, away from the center of rotation. From an inertial frame, no outward force exists — the object simply tends to move in a straight line due to inertia.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'Coriolis force' mean?

options.text: A pseudo-force that deflects moving objects in a rotating reference frame
A real contact force between two surfaces that causes friction effects
The gravitational force between two planets orbiting around each other
The tension force in a string that keeps an object spinning in circles

quiz_questions.explanation: The Coriolis force is a pseudo-force that affects objects moving within a rotating reference frame (like Earth). It deflects moving objects perpendicular to their velocity. On Earth, it deflects objects to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern Hemisphere.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'non-inertial' mean in the context of reference frames?

options.text: The reference frame is accelerating, so Newton's laws need modification
The reference frame has no mass and cannot support any physical objects
The reference frame is stationary and all objects within it are at rest
The reference frame only applies to objects that have zero net force

quiz_questions.explanation: Non-inertial means the reference frame is accelerating — either speeding up, slowing down, or rotating. In such frames, objects appear to experience forces that have no real source. These are the pseudo-forces we add to make Newton's laws work within the frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'center-fleeing' mean regarding centrifugal force?

options.text: The apparent force pushes objects away from the center of rotation
The real force pulls objects toward the center of the circular path
The force causes objects to stop rotating and move to the center
The force is perpendicular to both velocity and the rotation axis

quiz_questions.explanation: Centrifugal means 'center-fleeing' or 'away from center.' In a rotating frame, objects appear to be pushed outward (away from the center). This contrasts with centripetal ('center-seeking'), which is the real inward force keeping objects on a circular path.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'rotating reference frame' describe?

options.text: A coordinate system that spins around an axis, like being on a merry-go-round
A coordinate system that moves in a straight line at a constant velocity
A coordinate system that is completely stationary and does not move at all
A coordinate system that only measures the rotation of wheels or gears

quiz_questions.explanation: A rotating reference frame is a coordinate system that rotates around some axis. Earth itself is a rotating frame (it spins on its axis). A merry-go-round or spinning space station are other examples. In these frames, centrifugal and Coriolis pseudo-forces appear.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'apparent weight' mean?

options.text: The reading on a scale, which can differ from true weight in accelerating frames
The actual gravitational force on an object, which never changes with location
The mass of an object measured in kilograms on a standard balance scale
The weight of an object calculated using the formula W = mg at sea level

quiz_questions.explanation: Apparent weight is what a scale reads. In an accelerating elevator, a scale may read more or less than your true weight. In an upward-accelerating elevator, apparent weight increases. In a downward-accelerating elevator, it decreases. In free fall, apparent weight is zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'weightlessness' mean in physics?

options.text: A condition where apparent weight is zero because everything is in free fall
A condition where gravity does not exist at all in the surrounding area
A condition where an object has zero mass and therefore no gravitational pull
A condition that only occurs at infinite distance from all massive objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Weightlessness (or apparent weightlessness) occurs when an object and its surroundings are all in free fall together. Astronauts on the ISS experience weightlessness not because gravity is absent, but because they and the station are both falling toward Earth at the same rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the phrase 'frame of reference matters' mean in physics?

options.text: The description of motion depends on the observer's reference frame
Only one correct reference frame exists for describing any physical event
Reference frames do not affect the measurement of forces on objects
All observers always agree on the velocity and acceleration of objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Different reference frames can give different descriptions of the same event. A passenger in an accelerating car feels pushed backward (non-inertial frame), while an observer on the sidewalk sees the passenger's body resisting the change (inertial frame). Both descriptions are valid within their frames.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student stands in an elevator that accelerates upward. The student stands on a scale that normally reads 500 N. What does the scale read now?

options.text: Less than 500 N because the elevator moves away from the ground
Exactly 500 N because the student's weight has not changed at all
More than 500 N because the upward acceleration increases apparent weight
Zero because the elevator is accelerating and there is no contact force

quiz_questions.explanation: When the elevator accelerates upward, the scale must push harder to both support the student's weight and accelerate them upward. The apparent weight is W_apparent = m(g + a), which is greater than mg. The student feels heavier.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is in a car that turns sharply to the left. The student feels pushed to the right. What is the physics explanation from an inertial frame?

options.text: A real centrifugal force pushes the student outward to the right side
The student's inertia keeps the body moving straight while the car turns left
The car's engine creates a rightward force that acts on all passengers
Gravity shifts direction during the turn and pulls the student sideways

quiz_questions.explanation: From an inertial (outside) frame, no outward force exists. The student's body tends to continue in a straight line (Newton's First Law / inertia), while the car turns left beneath them. The student feels 'pushed' right, but it is really the car moving left.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student drops a ball inside an elevator that is in free fall. From the student's perspective inside the elevator, what does the ball appear to do?

options.text: Fall to the floor of the elevator at the normal rate of 9.8 m/s²
Float in place as if there were no gravity acting on it at all
Fly upward toward the ceiling of the elevator at a high speed
Move sideways toward the nearest wall of the elevator car

quiz_questions.explanation: In free fall, the elevator, the student, and the ball all accelerate downward at g together. From the student's (non-inertial) frame, the ball appears to float — this is apparent weightlessness. Gravity still acts, but everything falls together, so there is no relative acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student rides a spinning amusement park ride and feels pressed against the outer wall. From the rotating frame, what pseudo-force explains this sensation?

options.text: The Coriolis force deflects the student sideways along the wall
The centrifugal force pushes the student outward against the wall
The gravitational force increases due to the high speed of rotation
The normal force from the floor launches the student toward the wall

quiz_questions.explanation: In the rotating frame of the ride, the student feels the centrifugal pseudo-force pushing outward against the wall. From an inertial frame outside, the wall provides the centripetal (inward) force to keep the student moving in a circle, and the student's inertia makes them press against the wall.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student holds a pendulum inside a train. When the train accelerates forward, the pendulum swings backward. From the train's frame, what force caused the pendulum to swing?

options.text: A real backward force from the air conditioning system inside the train
A pseudo-force directed backward opposite to the train's acceleration
Gravity suddenly shifted direction when the train began to accelerate
The tension in the string decreased and allowed gravity to pull it back

quiz_questions.explanation: In the train's non-inertial frame, a pseudo-force F_pseudo = -ma_frame acts backward on everything, including the pendulum. This backward pseudo-force causes the pendulum to swing back. From an inertial frame, the pendulum simply maintains its position due to inertia while the train moves forward.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student in an elevator notices the scale reads 0 N. What can the student conclude about the elevator?

options.text: The elevator is moving upward at a constant high speed
The elevator is in free fall, accelerating downward at g
The elevator is stationary on the ground floor of the building
The elevator is accelerating upward faster than gravitational acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: A scale reading of 0 N means the apparent weight is zero — this occurs during free fall. The elevator and the student are both accelerating downward at g, so the student does not press against the scale. This is apparent weightlessness.

quiz_questions.question_text: A meteorologist explains that hurricanes in the Northern Hemisphere spin counterclockwise. A student asks what causes this rotation. What is the answer?

options.text: The Coriolis effect deflects winds to the right, creating counterclockwise rotation
Ocean currents underneath the storm push the air in a counterclockwise direction
Temperature differences between the poles and equator spin the storm that way
Earth's magnetic field deflects charged air particles into a spiral shape

quiz_questions.explanation: The Coriolis effect, caused by Earth's rotation, deflects moving air to the right in the Northern Hemisphere. As air rushes toward a low-pressure center, this rightward deflection causes the air to spiral counterclockwise around the center, forming the hurricane's characteristic rotation pattern.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places a cup of water on the dashboard of a car. When the car brakes suddenly, the water sloshes forward. From the car's frame, what pseudo-force acts on the water?

options.text: A backward pseudo-force because the car is decelerating (negative acceleration)
A forward pseudo-force because the car's deceleration creates a forward fictitious force
An upward pseudo-force that lifts the water out of the cup completely
No pseudo-force exists because braking is a real force on the car itself

quiz_questions.explanation: When the car brakes (decelerates forward, i.e., accelerates backward), the pseudo-force in the car's frame is F_pseudo = -m × a_frame. Since the frame accelerates backward, the pseudo-force points forward, pushing the water forward out of the cup.

quiz_questions.question_text: Astronauts on the International Space Station float around inside. A student asks if gravity has stopped working. What is the correct explanation?

options.text: Gravity is nearly zero at the altitude of the International Space Station
The station and astronauts are in free fall together, creating apparent weightlessness
The station's walls block gravity from reaching the astronauts floating inside
Special technology on the station cancels out the pull of Earth's gravity

quiz_questions.explanation: At the ISS altitude (~400 km), gravity is about 90% of its surface value. The astronauts float because the station and everyone inside are in continuous free fall (orbiting). They all accelerate toward Earth at the same rate, so there is no relative force between them — hence apparent weightlessness.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student sits on a rotating platform and tries to roll a ball straight outward. The ball curves to one side instead of going straight. What pseudo-force is responsible?

options.text: Centrifugal force pushing the ball directly outward from the center
The Coriolis force deflecting the ball perpendicular to its motion
Gravitational force pulling the ball downward toward the ground below
Friction from the platform surface slowing the ball and curving its path

quiz_questions.explanation: In the rotating frame, the Coriolis force acts perpendicular to the velocity of a moving object. When the ball moves outward, the Coriolis force deflects it sideways, causing it to curve instead of traveling in a straight line. From an inertial frame, the ball actually moves in a straight line — it is the platform that rotates underneath it.
